title: How to Steal Customers From Giants 600x Bigger than Your Startup | Pigment, Eléonore Crespo
summary: Eléonore Crespo, co-founder of Pigment, discusses how a Paris-based AI performance management platform competes against established enterprise software giants. She covers why legacy tools like spreadsheets fail at scale for large enterprises, the advantages of building a full platform from day one, and strategies for winning trust with marquee customers including Figma, Brex, and Anthropic.
writeup: Pigment co-founder Romain Gambier traces the founding logic of his AI performance management platform, which competes directly against entrenched legacy vendors SAP and Oracle. The core insight driving Pigment came from watching Google and elite VC-backed companies run strategic planning on spreadsheets: a tool built for quick models that collapses at enterprise scale when hundreds of business units, countries, and billions of rows must be unified. Pigment made a deliberate early bet to build a full enterprise-grade platform from day one rather than a narrow feature, spending two years in product development before launch, and used VC network credibility to unlock first customers including Figma and Brex.
key_points: Pigment raised over $400 million and has maintained a 2x ARR growth trajectory for consecutive years, serving CFOs and large finance teams as its primary buyer.

The founding insight came from observing that even Google used Google Sheets for strategic planning and revenue forecasting at scale, a tool that does not handle billions of rows across dozens of countries and hundreds of business units.

Because Pigment competes against 15- to 30-year-old legacy vendors that offer complete end-to-end platforms, the company concluded it could not launch a single feature and had to ship a full platform, including a custom computation engine called the elastic canvas and a purpose-built UX, which pushed the product timeline from late 2019 to 2021.

Pre-launch, Pigment raised $25 million on team strength alone, a strategy that required assembling a high-caliber engineering team plus former finance and revenue operations practitioners who could credibly endorse the product to prospective enterprise buyers.

Gambier's hiring framework centers on calibration (meeting the best candidates in each role before hiring to define what excellence looks like), rigorous case studies, and thorough background checks as the two most critical process stages.

Trust and social proof from fast-moving, well-regarded companies, specifically early customers like Figma and Brex who evangelized the platform publicly, created the compounding network effect that made enterprise sales progressively easier over time.
